does: This paper systematizes existing approaches to moral alignment in AI systems along a continuum from explicit hard-coded rules to purely learned implicit values. It analyzes the strengths and weaknesses of purely top-down deontological rule-based methods versus bottom-up learning approaches like learning from human feedback and LLM fine-tuning. The core contribution is arguing that effective moral alignment requires hybrid approaches combining both explicit and implicit components, integrating insights from multiple ethical frameworks including deontology, consequentialism, and virtue ethics to create AI systems that are simultaneously adaptable and robust while remaining controllable and interpretable.
connects: This work builds on decades of AI safety research concerned with value alignment and specification, extending recent discussions prompted by large language models and reinforcement learning from human feedback methods. It engages with classical AI approaches to encoding ethical constraints as formal rules or specifications, while also incorporating contemporary empirical learning methods developed for fine-tuning modern neural systems. The paper responds to recognized limitations in purely implicit learning approaches that struggle with interpretability and control, as well as brittle rule-based systems that lack adaptability. It synthesizes perspectives from machine ethics, value alignment literature, and philosophical approaches to ethics that have been increasingly studied in AI contexts.
enables: This manifesto positions hybrid moral alignment as a research direction that could lead to more trustworthy and robust autonomous systems. By articulating the space of possible approaches and their trade-offs, it enables future work to systematically explore combinations of explicit ethical principles with learned behavioral patterns. Such frameworks could improve interpretability and control of AI systems while maintaining the flexibility needed for real-world deployment. The work opens directions for developing formal methods that integrate multiple ethical theories, empirical studies comparing hybrid approaches, and techniques for verifying that combined explicit-implicit systems behave safely across diverse scenarios.
